title: Introduction to Java Streams
body:
Java Streams (Java 8+) provide a declarative, functional-style API for processing sequences of elements. A stream represents a pipeline of operations applied to a data source.

Key concepts:
- **Source** ‚Äî collection, array, file, generator
- **Intermediate operations** ‚Äî lazy, return a stream: `filter`, `map`, `sorted`, `distinct`, `limit`, `skip`, `peek`
- **Terminal operations** ‚Äî trigger evaluation: `collect`, `forEach`, `reduce`, `count`, `findFirst`, `anyMatch`

Streams are **lazy** ‚Äî intermediate operations are not executed until a terminal operation is called, and only the elements needed are processed.

Streams are **single-use** ‚Äî once a terminal operation is called, the stream is consumed and cannot be reused.

Use parallel streams (`parallelStream()`) for CPU-bound operations on large datasets, but be careful with shared mutable state.

examples.label: Basic stream pipeline

title: The Java Stream API Tutorial
body:
The Stream API offers a rich set of operations for data transformation.

**Mapping:**
- `map(fn)` ‚Äî transform each element
- `flatMap(fn)` ‚Äî flatten nested streams
- `mapToInt/Long/Double` ‚Äî convert to primitive streams

**Filtering/Slicing:**
- `filter(predicate)` ‚Äî keep matching elements
- `distinct()` ‚Äî remove duplicates
- `limit(n)` / `skip(n)` ‚Äî take/skip elements
- `takeWhile(p)` / `dropWhile(p)` ‚Äî Java 9+

**Reducing:**
- `reduce(identity, accumulator)` ‚Äî fold elements
- `count()`, `min()`, `max()`
- `sum()`, `average()` ‚Äî on numeric streams

**Collecting:**
- `collect(Collectors.toList())` ‚Äî to list
- `collect(Collectors.groupingBy(fn))` ‚Äî grouping
- `collect(Collectors.joining(", "))` ‚Äî string join

**Matching:**
- `anyMatch()`, `allMatch()`, `noneMatch()` ‚Äî boolean predicates
- `findFirst()`, `findAny()` ‚Äî Optional result

examples.label: flatMap and groupingBy

title: Applying Functional Interfaces in Java Streams

examples.label: Functional interfaces in streams

title: Guide to Java Collectors
body:
`Collectors` is a utility class providing implementations of the `Collector` interface used with `Stream.collect()`.

**Collecting to collections:**
- `toList()` ‚Äî to mutable List
- `toUnmodifiableList()` ‚Äî immutable
- `toSet()` ‚Äî to HashSet
- `toMap(keyFn, valueFn)` ‚Äî to HashMap
- `toCollection(supplier)` ‚Äî to any collection

**Grouping and partitioning:**
- `groupingBy(classifier)` ‚Äî Map<K, List<T>>
- `groupingBy(classifier, downstream)` ‚Äî nested collectors
- `partitioningBy(predicate)` ‚Äî Map<Boolean, List<T>>

**Summarizing:**
- `counting()` ‚Äî count of elements
- `summingInt/Long/Double(fn)` ‚Äî sum
- `averagingDouble(fn)` ‚Äî average
- `summarizingInt(fn)` ‚Äî min, max, sum, count, average

**Joining:**
- `joining()` ‚Äî concatenate strings
- `joining(delimiter, prefix, suffix)`

examples.label: groupingBy with downstream collector

title: Java Stream Filter with Lambda Expression
body:
`Stream.filter(predicate)` is an intermediate operation that keeps only elements matching the given predicate.

```java
stream.filter(e -> e.getSalary() > 50000)
```

The predicate is any `Predicate<T>` ‚Äî a lambda, method reference, or composed predicate.

**Filtering with multiple conditions:**
```java
.filter(e -> e.getSalary() > 50000 && e.getDept().equals("Engineering"))
// or using Predicate composition:
Predicate<Employee> highPaid = e -> e.getSalary() > 50000;
Predicate<Employee> isEngineer = e -> e.getDept().equals("Engineering");
.filter(highPaid.and(isEngineer))
```

**Filtering nulls:**
```java
.filter(Objects::nonNull)
```

`filter` is lazy ‚Äî it only evaluates the predicate when the terminal operation demands the next element. This enables short-circuit evaluation with `findFirst()` or `anyMatch()`.

examples.label: Filter with Predicate composition
